coredata.dc:title: Sustainable synthesis of carbon dots from agarose waste and prospective application in sensing of l-aspartic acid
coredata.dc:creator: Chauhan, Pooja
Saini, Jasmine
Chaudhary, Savita
Bhasin, K.K.
coredata.dc:description: The excessive utilization of carbon dots (CDs) has led to different fates and perseverance which further prejudiced by various photochemical and other transformations. The mechanistic behaviour of their respective conversions is still very unclear. The present report fulfils this knowledge breach by single step synthesis of CDs with one step synthesis of CDs by thermal treatment by using agarose waste. The formed particles have possessed outstanding quantum yield with value ∼ 62 %. The developed CDs are highly sustainable and non-toxic in nature as compared to organic and inorganic quantum dots as their unknown chemical, physical and optoelectronic properties limit their widespread use. The exceptional luminescence and physicochemical characteristics of developed CDs inspired this research work towards commercial products. With this view, they particles have been successfully applied in recognition of biological component such as aspartic acid. The fluorescence emission intensity of developed CDs reflects extraordinary sensitivity and selectivity towards l-aspartic acid out of twenty amino acids through quenching mechanism in wide concentration range with a linear relationship of 10 μm to 1 nm and value of detection limit was found to be 0.28 nm. Astonishingly, such a sensor can be applied in real world analysis with satisfactory spiked recovery of 98 %. The developed C-dots are pragmatic to real sample recognition and results are highly sustaining.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Aspartic acid
Quenching
Amino acid
Sensing and fluorescence

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Thermal treatment of waste agarose gel to fabricate CDs
Recognition of l-Asp and its mechanism
Optical and structural characterization of CDs
Fluorescence sensing ability of developed CDs towards aspartic acid

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Haryana State Council for Science and Technology

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration showing the synthesis of CDs from waste agar gel.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum, (b) fluorescence emission spectra showing the excitation dependent variations, (c) concentration dependent changes, (d) value of quantum yield as a function of excitation wavelength, (e) effect of pH variation and (f) time dependent fluorescence intensity variation with changes in intensity as a function of ionic strength variation (inset) of prepared CDs.
(a) FTIR spectrum, (b) FESEM image, (c) elemental analysis, (d) TGA with DTA curves and (e) DSC graph of CDs derived from waste agar gel.
(a) Fluorescence spectrum of CDs in presence of different amino acids, (b) concentration variation emission spectrum of l-Asp and (c) stern-Volmer plot for the developed CDs.
(a) Interference study of l-Asp in presence of several amino acids and (b) normalized intensity plot of developed CDs in presence of various amino acids.
Schematic illustration showing the interaction of CDs with l-Asp.
Recovery study of developed CDs in presence of different concentration of l-Asp in contact of different water mediums.

head.ce:title: Sustainable synthesis of carbon dots from agarose waste and prospective application in sensing of l-aspartic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Centre of Advanced Studies in Chemistry, Panjab University, Chandigarh, 160014, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Centre of Advanced Studies in Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry and Centre of Advanced Studies in Chemistry, Panjab University, Chandigarh 160014, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The excessive utilization of carbon dots (CDs) has led to different fates and perseverance which further prejudiced by various photochemical and other transformations. The mechanistic behaviour of their respective conversions is still very unclear. The present report fulfils this knowledge breach by single step synthesis of CDs with one step synthesis of CDs by thermal treatment by using agarose waste. The formed particles have possessed outstanding quantum yield with value ∼ 62 %. The developed CDs are highly sustainable and non-toxic in nature as compared to organic and inorganic quantum dots as their unknown chemical, physical and optoelectronic properties limit their widespread use. The exceptional luminescence and physicochemical characteristics of developed CDs inspired this research work towards commercial products. With this view, they particles have been successfully applied in recognition of biological component such as aspartic acid. The fluorescence emission intensity of developed CDs reflects extraordinary sensitivity and selectivity towards l-aspartic acid out of twenty amino acids through quenching mechanism in wide concentration range with a linear relationship of 10 μm to 1 nm and value of detection limit was found to be 0.28 nm. Astonishingly, such a sensor can be applied in real world analysis with satisfactory spiked recovery of 98 %. The developed C-dots are pragmatic to real sample recognition and results are highly sustaining.

ce:section.ce:para: Out of different types of available amino acids, aspartic acid (L-Asp) is considered as one of the prime proteinogenic amino acid which executes imperative role in living organisms and biosynthesis of various types of other amino acids [1–4]. l-Asp has produced strong stimulating effect on the cerebral cortex which is strongly related to the cognition and thinking process in living beings [5,6]. Currently, l-Asp has also been scrutinized for their role in regulating the hormone in living beings [7]. Contrarily, the abnormal level of l-Asp is associated with various kinds of disorders such as epilepsy, Lou Gehrig's disease, stroke and osteoarthritis [8,9]. The excessive amount of l-Asp has also related with the early-onset of dementia and neurological disease like Alzheimer’s [10]. Due to the aforesaid importance of l-Asp in living system, the selective identification towards l-Asp is recently attaining higher connotation [11]. Till date, diverse range of methods such as capillary electrophoresis (CE), chromatographic and electrochemical methods have been used for the quantification of l-Asp [12–17]. But their complicated and expensive working has limited their widespread usage in sensing. Thus, there is an urgent demand of developing economically viable sensor for the detection of l-Asp.

In this outlook, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have acquired special consideration due to their outstanding sensitivity and quick response time [1,14]. The advanced properties of CDs such as superior luminescence activities, high photo stability, excellent aqueous dispersion ability with lower lethal impact for generating an effective fluorophore has enhanced their ability towards sensing [18–20]. CDs have exhibited a series of advantages including biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity as well as excellent electronic and optical properties [3]. They have numerous potential applications in bioimaging, diagnosis, drug delivery, photo-catalysis and fluorescence sensing. CDs are especially designed and developed as the sensors for the detecting small molecules such as drugs, biomolecules, ions, environmental pollutants and amino acids [19]. Till date, there are various available approaches such as laser ablation, hydrothermal, microwave, pyrolysis etc has been utilized for the synthesis of CDs. Out of various synthetic methodologies, green synthesis using fruits peel, table sugar, Konjac flour, kitchen waste and many more is considered as the most cost effective and economically viable approach for the development of highly dispersed, small sized and fluorescent CDs with very high quantum yield values [21–24]. The nature of the stating material has displayed a significant impact over the optical and emission properties of formed CDs [25–27]. For instance, Ansi et al. have synthesized CDs derived from table sugar for effective sensing of lead ions with naked eye at ppb level in drinking water [25]. Ma et al. have synthesized highly fluorescent boron doped CDs for the sensitive sensing of catechol and glutathione under high concentrations in presence of various interfering substances [28]. Jana et al. have prepared biocompatible nitrogen doped CDs for fluorescence-based detection of nitrite ion at nM level in different water mediums [29]. The ability of CDs for discriminating various analytes with higher selectivity and accuracy are well documented in literature, but limited data have been reported for selective sensing of l-Asp. Therefore, the usage of fast, biocompatible, selective and sensitive CDs based sensor for the identification of l-Asp is highly advantageous.

In this prospective, the current work illustrates the transformation of waste agar gel into highly water-soluble fluorescent CDs by thermal treatment. The agar gel is one of the most abundant and inexpensive biomass and rich source of polysaccharides. However, most part of its disposal required expensive treatment, which causes a grave hazard to the living beings [30,31]. The current strategy for converting bio-waste into highly advanced CDs has owed to the green and sustainable improvement by upcycling of agar waste. The as formed CDs have possessed high quantum yield (ɸ) of 62 %, and display a noticeable fluorescence quenching toward l-Asp, portraying them ideal sensors for the quantification of l-Asp. Additionally, the sensing ability of formed CDs has further been extended in real water samples. The current work might offer an inspiration to associates the use of new carbon sources to fabricate fluorescent particles by employing economically viable materials and provide a new way for scrutinizing l-Asp in aqueous media.

Muller Hinton (MH, Himedia with purity >98 %) agar was used for gel preparation in antimicrobial studies. Agarose (Polysaccharide) powder from Himedia with 99 % purity has been used for electrophoresis studies. All the respective salts of used l-amino acids i.e. aspartic acid, asparagine, glutamine, tyrosine, leucine, isoleucine, histidine, alanine, arginine, cysteine, glutamic acid, lysine, methionine, phenylalanine, proline, serine, threonine, tryptophan, valine and glycine were procured from Sigma-Aldrich with HPLC grade having 98 % purity. NaCl and KCl were procured from Sigma-Aldrich of analytical grade with 99 % purity. Pellets of NaOH and conc. HCl were purchased from Fisher Scientific with analytical status (99 % purity). All the chemicals were used as such without doing any further purification. Deionised water was used as a dispersing solvent medium for the preparation of all the samples. To carry out the recovery study, different water samples were collected from different sources such as tap water and rain water were procured from Panjab University, Chandigarh. Lake water was taken from Sukhna Lake, Chandigarh. Well water was collected from the village Piyala, Dist. Faridabad, Haryana, India.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal treatment of waste agarose gel to fabricate CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The waste over agarose gel obtained after carrying out the microbial studies has been utilized for the preparation of thermally-calcined CDs (Scheme 1 ). To synthesize CDs, 10 g of agar waste was placed in closed lid silica crucible and allowed to heat in muffle furnace at uniform temperature of 450 °C for 4 h. After completion, the furnace was allowed to cool at room temperature and crucibles were removed out from it. The obtained blackish-brown powder was allowed to grind by electrical pestle-mortar and further sieved through 0.18 mm mesh size for removing bigger sized particles. Afterwards, 1 g obtained powder was dispersed in 200 mL deionised water and allowed for 24 h at room temperature. The formed solution was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 8 min. The obtained supernatant was allowed to heat at 120 °C for 1 h to obtain powder form of CDs. The obtained black-grey powder of CDs was allowed to keep at 5 °C for further characterization. The preparation of CDs was also carried out at different temperature range 200–500 °C (Fig. S1 and Table S1) for preliminary studies.

The absorption behaviour of developed CDs was analysed by using LABINDIA UV–vis. spectrophotometer with 1 cm quartz cell having fixed path length of 2.0 cm. The Power Sonic 405 Sonicator was used for preparing homogenous solution of CDs in presence of various amino acids. Mettler Toledo digital pH meter was utilized to check the acidic and basic nature of formed CDs. Thermogravimetric analysis (TG-DTA) was carried in working range of 0−1000 °C on SDT Q-600 instrument. Mettler Toledo Q20 differential scanning calorimetric (DSC) instrument in working range of 0−350 °C under nitrogen environment was employed to investigate the thermal behaviour of developed CDs. Thermo Scientific iS50 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR) was employed to study the nature of exterior surface of formed CDs. FE-SEM instrument (JEOLJSM 7500 F) with energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) was utilized to explore the morphological alterations, purity and elemental mapping of formed CDs at 20 Kev. Panalytical X’Pert Pro X-ray powder diffractometer was utilized to analyse the crystalline structure of developed CDs under monochromatic radiation with 2θ range of 5−90° and average scan range of 2deg/min. The value of zeta-potential was evaluated on Anton Paar Litesizer 500 instrument with Omega (cuvette) measurement cell. Perkin Elmer LS-55 Spectro-fluorometric instrument in 200−800 nm working range was utilized to explore the emission profile of prepared CDs in presence of different amino acids. During synthesis, the development of CDs was carried out at different temperature such as 200, 250, 300, 350, 400, 450 and 500 °C for preliminary studies. The fluorescence spectrum of developed CDs was recorded and it was observed that on increasing the temperature from 200 to 450 °C, the value of emission intensity increases. However, the value of emission intensity starts decreasing after 500 °C (Fig. S1). Similarly, their DLS analysis was carried out for the CDs in temperature range 200−500 °C (Table S1) to measure their hydrodynamic radii. Further, CDs prepared at 450 °C were utilized for the investigation of toxicity assessment and sensing purpose of amino acids. The value of quantum yield (ɸ) for the developed CDs was determined by considering Rhodamine B as a reference standard material [32].The limit of detection (LOD) and quantitation limit (LQ) for the as-synthesized CDs in presence of l-Asp acid was further determined from stern-Volmer plot (F/Fo vs. [L-Asp]). Further, the fluorescence quenching factor (Fo-F/ Fo) was obtained from s-V plot [33]. The value of binding constant was calculated out from Benesi-Hilderbrand plot (1/F-Fo vs. 1/[L-Asp]) after applying the following formula [34].
(1) LOD = 3SD/P
(2) LQ = 10SD/P
(3) Binding constant = Slope/Intercept
Where Fo refers to the fluorescence emission intensity of pure CDs solution, F refers to the emission intensity of CDs in presence of l-Asp, S.D. refers to the standard deviation of sensing system obtained from s-V plots and P corresponds to the slope value of linear fit.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recognition of l-Asp and its mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: The chemosensing application of prepared CDs against different types of l-amino acids i.e. aspartic acid, asparagine, glutamine, tyrosine, leucine, isoleucine, histidine, alanine, arginine, cysteine, glutamic acid, lysine, methionine, phenylalanine, proline, serine, threonine, tryptophan, valine and glycine were checked. To scrutinize the potential of CDs towards amino acid sensing, equi-volume mixture of CDs and amino acid (1:1) was utilized during analysis. Briefly explaining, the fluorescence spectrum of incubated equi-volume solution of different amino acids with aqueous solution of CDs at room temperature was recorded in range 200−800 nm. In addition, the fluorescence selectivity study of CDs towards l-Asp was checked by keeping the fixed concentration of CDs in presence of different concentrations of l-Asp ranging from 105 to 1 nM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical and structural characterization of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The dried form of agarose gel is mainly a linear polymer formed by the polymerization of a disaccharide namely agarobiose. Being rich in C, H and O elements, agarose gel was considered as one of the important carbon precursors for the synthesis of CDs. A blackish brown dispersion of CDs was attained by thermal treatment of waste agar in muffle furnace, and following refinement via ultra centrifugation. The aqueous solution of formed CDs has reflected milky coloration under normal visible light. However, oceanic blue color was reflected by CDs under UV illumination (Fig. 1 a). The optoelectronic properties of as formed CDs were primarily associated with the π-plasmon excitation present in the carbon core structure and their conjugation with the different types of transition states present in the functional moieties over the exterior surface of CDs. For instance, the absorbance band at 230 nm was aroused due to the presence of polyaromatic chromophoric units with sp2 cluster of π-conjugated bonds in formed CDs. However, the peak at 290 nm has been corresponded to π-π* and n-π* transition of different functional groups existed over the exterior surface of CDs respectively. Fig. 1b demonstrates an excitation-dependent fluorescence emission behaviour of agarose derived CDs. The emission peak was found to be red shifted on enhancing the excitation wavelength from 300 to 360 nm. However, the emission intensity was found to be decreased with increase in the excitation wavelength and maxima was found with 300 nm. These changes in emission intensity were mainly related with the presence of different sized CDs in the samples. However, the existence of different types of emissive states in formed CDs was also related with such emission behaviour. The concentration dependent emission behaviour from 0.5 mg/mL to 2.5 mg/mL of CDs has shown linear intensity enhancement upto 2 mg/mL (Fig. 1c). However, further increasing the concentration has displayed significant decrease in emission intensity of CDs. Hence the results suggested that 2 mg/mL concentration of CDs was appropriate for carrying out their emission studies.

In addition, the excitation dependent quantum yield of formed particles has displayed maximum value of 62 % at 300 nm (Fig. 1d). The variation was related with the nature of the surface defects and size of formed CDs from waste agarose gel. The stability of the formed particles was further tested in different pH media ranging from 2.2 to 12.6 (Fig. 1e). Form the data; it was observed that the fluorescence emission was decreasing on enhancing the pH from 2.2 to 4.4. However, on further increasing the pH upto 12.6 has portrayed linear enhancement in fluorescence emission intensity. The results have suggested the extreme stability of CDs in basic medium. Further, the long-term steadiness of developed CDs was examined by comparing the time-dependent fluorescence emission intensity under room temperature conditions. The formed CDs have exhibited exceptionally high stability over 24 h (Fig. 1f). The slight variation in emission intensity has further suggested the excellent photostability of CDs. The stability was mainly acquired from the existing functional groups over the surface of CDs [35]. The effect of ionic strength on fluorescence emission intensity of formed CDs as a function of concentration was assessed by using the application of NaCl and KCl salts (Fig. 1f inset). From the data, it has been observed that the emission intensity of formed CDs has shown noticeable enhancement on increasing the concentration of ionic salts. Its behavioural aspect was explained due to the augmentation in the number of ionic species around the surface of formed CDs. Therefore, the excellent photostability, outstanding fluorescence emission behaviour in different physiological mediums and enhanced aptitude at different concentration of ionic strength (NaCl, KCl) made the developed CDs as a potential candidate in biological field.

FTIR analysis has been carried out to appraise the existence of diverse functional groups on the exterior surface of CDs (Fig. 2 a). In FTIR spectrum, three major and intense peaks were observed at 1630 cm−1, 2142 cm−1 and 3272 cm-1 and one minor peak was found at 3775 cm−1 where band at 1630 cm−1 has been corresponded to the carbonyl (-C = O) functional group of carboxylated moieties in CDs [8]. The respective peak at 2142 cm−1 has been referred to the -C-H stretching. The band at 3272 cm−1 corresponds to the strong hydroxyl (−OH) and amine (NH) group present in CDs [9,36]. The FTIR characterization approves the presence of different functional groups on the surface of CDs. These polar functional groups suggest the excellent water solubility and strong affinity of CDs toward various amino acids. Further, CHNS elemental analysis have confirmed the presence of 52.80, 26.98, 8.12 and 12.10 % of carbon (C), oxygen (O), nitrogen (N) and hydrogen (H) in the formed CDs. The data has supported the presence of oxygen and nitrogen containing functional group over the surface of CDs and made them highly water soluble and strong linker for different bio molecules. The formed particles have also displayed the lamellar porous morphology (Fig. 2b) with highly conglomerated nature in solid state. The FESEM analysis of post centrifugation sample and final reaction product has been compared to evaluate their morphological alterations (Fig. S2). It was observed that final reaction product (developed CDs) has more uniform and clear lamellar morphology as compared to other. The elemental mapping has been carried out (Fig. 2c). The zeta potential value of formed CDs has been found to be 26.2 mV at 25 °C with 200 V (Fig. S3). The value of electrophoretic mobility, conductivity, optical density and transmittance for the developed CDs was found to be 2.0443 μm.cm/Vs, 2.497 mS/cm, 2.96 and 82 % respectively. Whereas, the viscosity, relative permittivity and refractive index of solvent media was found 0.8903 mPa.s, 78.37 and 1.33 respectively.

The hydrodynamic radii for the developed CDs have been evaluated by using DLS analysis at different temperature range (Table S1). The smaller size for developed CDs was obtained at 450 °C with PDI value of 0.32 (Fig. S4). The outcomes suggested the formation of uniform particle size at 450 °C. Simultaneously, the DLS spectrum of CDs obtained at temperature range of 200, 250, 300, 350, 400 and 500 °C has been represented in Fig. S5. From here it can be concluded that developed CDs have exhibited exceptional size variations as a function of temperature with minimum obtained size was at 450 °C. The crystalline spectra of prepared CDs (at 450 °C) have displayed major peaks at 2θ = 29.45°, 39.43°, 49.28°, 58.52° and 69.38° which corresponds to the (002), (111), (200), (600) and (220) plane (Fig. S6). The broad peak around 2θ = 29° has confirmed the carbonaceous core of developed CDs material. On the other hand, the peak at 2θ = 45° can be ascribed to the graphitic nature of formed CDs. The increase in interplanar spacing (d) value has further suggested the amorphous nature of CDs. This was mainly aroused due to the presence of oxygen containing functional group moieties over the surface of formed CDs. The size of formed CDs was come out to be 5.9 nm. The value of dislocation density (ʆ) was found to be 0.0287 nm−1. The outcomes have suggested the increment in number of unit cell due to increase number of defects in amorphous form of CDs. Further, to explain their structural characterization and topographical imaging, HRTEM analysis has been carried out (Fig. S7). From the obtained spectrum, it was observed that developed CDs have uniform, spherical and smaller size particles with size range 2−10 nm along with planar spacing. Fig. S7b has displayed the Raman spectrum of the synthesized CDs. The spectrum has shown the major Raman bands at 1457.8 and 1662.8 cm-1, which correlates to sp2 carbon (G-band). In addition, the together with sp3 defects (D-band) has also been observed from these spectral peaks in prepared CDs.

The thermal decomposition behaviour of CDs has been investigated by carrying out the TGA analysis in temperature range of 0−1000 °C (Fig. 2d). The three major weight losses of 8.3, 21.8 and 19.5 % have been observed at 138.33 °C, 756.25 °C and 987.96 °C respectively. The first mass loss was mainly occurred due to the evaporation of moisture in CDs and breakage of loosely bounded water molecules present on the exterior surface of CDs. The second mass loss was mainly associated with the removal of different exterior functional groups from the surface of CDs. The third mass loss was probably aroused due to release of pyro-gases in CDs [36]. DSC studies have displayed the existence of exothermic behaviour in formed CDs (Fig. 2e) with the heat of enthalpy value was 36.36 J/g. The respective value of glass transition temperature and melting temperature was around 154.26 °C and 187.87 °C respectively. The value of onset temperature was found to be 54.56 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing ability of developed CDs towards aspartic acid

ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the practical usage of formed CDs, the sensing aptitude of amino acid was performed by using the fluorescence spectroscopic measurements. During sample analysis, equi-volume mixture of CDs and amino acid (1:1) was utilized and their fluorescence spectrum was recorded at 300 nm excitation (Fig. 3 a). From the series of selected amino acids (aspartic acid, asparagine, glutamine, tyrosine, leucine, isoleucine, histidine, alanine, arginine, cysteine, glutamic acid, lysine, methionine, phenylalanine, proline, serine, threonine, tryptophan, valine and glycine (Fig. S8)), the developed CDs have reflected higher selectivity towards l-Asp. Being one of the significant amino acid, l-Asp played decisive role in continuing ordinary and biological functioning of human body. l-Asp is considered as the foremost neurotransmitter excitors of central nervous system, but its redundant amount cause epilepsy and strokes. Therefore, the scrutiny and recognition of l-Asp is of great significance. Till date, various analytical methods have been reported for the selective detection of l-Asp such as electrochemical method, chromatography, and spectrophotometric methods. Out of these, spectrophotometric method especially fluorescent based is considered as the most easy, suitable and fast approach for the detection of biomolecules.

The developed CDs are considered as highly fluorescent materials and act as effective material for the detection of amino acid. Under the most favourable situations, the fluorescence intensity of formed CDs at 300 nm excitation has decreased in a ratio of 73.1 % in the presence of l-Asp as compared to the other chosen amino acids. Fig. 3b demonstrated the regular decrease of fluorescence intensity of CDs upon addition of l-Asp concentrations ranging from 1 nm to10 μm. On decreasing the concentration of l-Asp from 10 μm to 1 nm, the emission intensity increases linearly. It was observed that in presence of 1 nm amount of l-Asp, the emission intensity was matched with the emission intensity of pure CDs. From here it can be concluded that 1 nM is minimum concentration upto which formed CDs has shown ultimate selectivity for l-Asp. The value of detection and quantification limit was found 0.28 nM and 0.95 nM which were calculated from stern-Volmer plot (Fig. 3c) for l-Asp. The value of binding constant was 0.109 nM evaluated from Benesi-Hilderbrand plot (Fig. S9). From here it can be inferred that the developed CDs reflected eventual discrimination and sensitivity towards l-Asp out under extreme concentration level.

To inspect the reproducibility of developed CDs sensor, all the experimentation has been performed three times. The potential selectivity of prepared CDs towards l-Asp was evaluated. The different amino acids such as asparagine, glutamine, tyrosine, leucine, isoleucine, histidine, alanine, arginine, cysteine, glutamic acid, lysine, methionine, phenylalanine, proline, serine, threonine, tryptophan, valine and glycine were mixed with aqueous suspension of CDs and l-Asp. The respective interference study has been carried out for the selectivity analysis (Fig. 4 a). From the emission interference spectrum, it was observed that the emission intensity of CDs and l-Asp solution does not changes much in presence of other amino acids and supported the potential selectivity of CDs towards l-Asp in presences of various biomolecules. Further, normalized intensity plot was drawn from interference study graph to scrutinize the selectivity of CDs (Fig. 4b). In general, admirable water solubility and different polar surface functional groups on CDs provides various active sites, high sensitivity and selectivity towards analyte.

The main reason behind the ultimate sensing of l-Asp towards CDs has been aroused due to the collision between the fluorophoric group of CDs and quencher (L-Asp). This collision has the tendency to produce the non-fluorescent ground state complex via the transference of energy and molecular rearrangement (Fig. 5 ). This complexation has further caused the accumulation of molecules and augmented the intra-molecular charge transfer (ICT) between the surface functional group of CDs and l-Asp through hydrogen bonding. This results in the exponential quenching selectivity towards l-Asp which suggests that energy of absorption is greater than energy of emission and leads to shortened life time. Simultaneously, complete charge transfer process also takes place when system is promoted from ground state to first excited state. In contrast to the earlier reports for l-Asp determination (Table S2), our method joins the advantages of higher sensitivity and exceptionally high specificity towards l-Asp. The developed sensor has very high value of detection limit and binding constant without utilizing any organic solvent and expensive instrumentation when compared to the reported literature data. Similarly, developed CDs displayed excellent value of quantum yield (62 %) which suggests their outstanding absorption and emission properties. The low fabrication cost and convenient recycling of agar waste to highly fluorescent material has further enhanced the scope of the product in environmental remediation activities.

By employing the standard addition technique, the practical applicability of developed CDs for l-Asp has been checked in different sources of real water samples including rain water, tap water, lake water and deionised water (Fig. 6 ). The utilized samples were spiked with different concentration of l-Asp (2, 4, 6, 8 and 10 nM) and their fluorescence emission profile have been recorded in presence of CDs via exciting the samples with λex. = 300 nm. The fluorescence emission intensity of CDs has displayed noticeable quenching in each case. The recovery percentage was found to be ranging from 95 to 98 % with relative standard deviation (RSD) as low as 2.3 %. The outcomes have clearly implied that the suggested fluorescence methodology is quite precise and reproducible and grasps an important role for sensing l-Asp in real water samples.

In conclusion, an effort has been made for the fabrication of CDs by simple thermal treatment of waste agarose gel. This methodology has not required expensive reagents and considered inexpensive, easy to prepared and environmentally reliable for converting waste to wealth. The as-prepared CDs have possessed very high quantum yield of 62 % and display a specific fluorescence response to l-Asp via quenching of emission intensity, which makes them capable sensor for l-Asp analysis. The linear range of analysis was found to be in range of 1 nm to10 μm, and the LOD is as low as 0.28 nM. Using these CDs, the estimation of l-Asp in different types of real water samples accomplishes adequate outcomes. The through consideration has proposed that fluorescence induced electron transference between CDs and l-Asp is responsible for the fluorescence “turn-off” mechanism in sensing. The substantial quenching of developed CD’s will open an innovative avenue to enterprise novel aspartic acid sensor in real world analysis.

Pooja Chauhan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Jasmine Saini: Software, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation. Savita Chaudhary: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Resources, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. K.K. Bhasin: Writing - review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts of interest to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Savita Chaudhary is thankful to DST Inspire Faculty award [IFACH- 17], Haryana State Council for Science and Technology [HSCSIT/R&D/2020/476] and DST Purse grants II for financial assistance. Pooja Chauhan is thankful to Open SRF CSIR fellowship 2020. Authors are also thankful to Panjab University SAIF CIL and Department of Environment Studies Panjab University Chandigarh for technical support. We are also dedicating this work in fond memory of Prof. R.C. Paul.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.materresbull.2020.111113.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fabrication of iron modified screen printed carbon electrode for sensing of amino acids
Polyhedron.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Tayyab Raza Naqvi, T. Rasheed, M. Naeem Ashiq, M. Najam ul Haq, S. Majeed, B. Fatima, R. Nawaz, D. Hussain, S. Shafi, Fabrication of iron modified screen printed carbon electrode for sensing of amino acids, Polyhedron. 180 (2020) 114426. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.poly.2020.114426.

Angew. Chemie Int. Ed. Eng.
